questions.question: An aluminum housing arrives with light machining oil over the test area, which was also spot-painted. Correct preparation for PT is to:
options: Vapor degrease only - the degreaser will lift the paint too
Strip the coating from the area of interest, remove the oil (e.g., solvent or vapor degrease), then dry completely before penetrant
Wire brush through the paint to expose bright metal, then test
Apply extra penetrant so it soaks through the paint
questions.explanation: Coatings bridge and seal flaw openings and must be stripped from the area of interest; oils are then removed by an appropriate cleaning method, and the part must be dry before application. Brushing would smear metal over flaw mouths.

questions.question: For a fatigue-critical machined part, vapor degreasing is preferred over abrasive blasting as the final cleaning step because:
options: Degreasing is cheaper per part
Blasting cools the part below the temperature band
Degreasing removes oils without mechanically smearing metal over crack openings, which blasting can do
Blast media chemically reacts with penetrant dyes
questions.explanation: Abrasive blasting peens ductile surface metal across flaw mouths and can seal tight cracks shut; chemical cleaning removes soils while leaving flaw openings intact.

questions.question: Why do inspectors wait several minutes inside the darkened booth before evaluating fluorescent indications?
options: To let the UV lamp reach full output
To let the developer finish drying
To let any remaining wash water evaporate
To let their eyes dark-adapt so dim indications and faint background become visible
questions.explanation: Eyes coming from white light need several minutes of dark adaptation before they can perceive low-brightness fluorescent indications reliably - a typical procedure requirement for booth work.

questions.question: A part measures 35 °F on a winter morning. Under typical procedure values, you should:
options: Apply penetrant and use the standard dwell
Warm the part and materials into the standard band (about 40-125 °F), or use a procedure qualified for low-temperature testing
Apply penetrant and halve the dwell to limit evaporation
Substitute developer dwell for penetrant dwell
questions.explanation: Standard penetrant systems are qualified within roughly 40-125 °F. Below the band the options are to bring the part into range or use a system specifically qualified for the lower temperature.

questions.question: On an immersion line, most of the penetrant dwell is commonly run as drain dwell because draining on the rack:
options: Keeps a wet working film on the surfaces while conserving penetrant and preventing pooling
Cools the part into the temperature band faster
Removes the need for a wash step afterward
Hardens the penetrant film for easier handling
questions.explanation: After dipping, the wet film keeps feeding flaws while excess penetrant runs back to the tank - dwell continues, material is saved, and puddles that complicate removal are avoided.

questions.question: Which change to a Method A wash most increases the risk of stripping penetrant out of flaws?
options: Lowering water pressure from 40 to 30 psi
Holding the spray at an angle to the surface
Switching from a coarse droplet spray to a concentrated needle jet at higher pressure
Washing under the UV-A lamp instead of white light
questions.explanation: A tight high-pressure jet drives water directly into discontinuities and flushes out the trapped penetrant. Coarse droplets at modest pressure and an angle clean the surface while sparing the flaws.

questions.question: Hydrophilic (Method D) removal is generally preferred for high-sensitivity fluorescent inspection of critical hardware because:
options: It skips the final rinse and saves time
It uses no water, eliminating drying
The emulsifier is applied at full strength, removing background fastest
Each stage - pre-rinse, diluted emulsifier at a controlled concentration, final rinse - is separately controllable, minimizing the chance of stripping flaws
questions.explanation: Method D divides removal into independently controlled steps, with bath concentration (typically 5-35% for dip) verified by refractometer - controlled removal is exactly what high-sensitivity penetrants require.

questions.question: A Method B (lipophilic) line accidentally leaves parts in the emulsifier for twice the qualified contact time before rinsing. The most likely result is:
options: No effect - the rinse stops all emulsifier action retroactively
Emulsifier diffused into flaw-entrapped penetrant, so it rinsed away and indications are lost - the parts must be reprocessed
Stronger indications, since more penetrant was mobilized
Heavy background requiring only a longer developer time
questions.explanation: Lipophilic emulsifier works by progressive diffusion into the penetrant layer. Doubled contact lets it reach the penetrant inside flaws, which then washes out with the excess - an overwash produced chemically.

questions.question: During a fluorescent Method A wash, the operator works under ordinary white shop light instead of the station's UV-A lamp. The process risk is that the operator:
options: Cannot see the fluorescent background, so there is no endpoint gauge - the part may be underwashed or overwashed
Will be dazzled by the fluorescence of the penetrant
Will exceed the water temperature limit
Cannot verify the water pressure without UV light
questions.explanation: On fluorescent lines the fading background glow under UV-A is how the wash endpoint is judged. Without it the operator is washing blind - stopping too early leaves background, stopping too late strips flaws.

questions.question: After processing, a weld shows faint, broken indications along its toe where clear cracking was expected. Which combination of process records would best explain the result?
options: Dwell 15 minutes, film kept wet, background cleared at 30 seconds of washing
White light 110 fc, part temperature 75 °F, coarse spray at 35 psi
Dwell logged at 4 minutes on a 55 °F part, followed by an extended wash 'to be sure'
Solvent pre-clean with 10 minutes flash-off, brush application with a 1-inch overlap band
questions.explanation: Faint, broken indications point to partially filled or partially stripped flaws: a short dwell on a cold part underfills the cracks, and washing past background strips what little entered. The other records describe a healthy process.
